messages.content:
In the 9th century CE, slaves from East Africa led this guerrilla war that lasted for fourteen years and nearly toppled the Abbasid Caliphate.

Question 1 options:

A) Zanj Rebellion


B) Fatimid Revolt


C) Third Servile War


D)Yanga Insurrection
A) Zanj Rebellion

What role did Berbers play in the Western Sudanic empires?

Question 2 options:

A) To finance their military campaigns, the kings of the Western Sudan enslaved and sold the Berbers.


B) The Berbers were traders who crossed the desert; they introduced Islam to market towns in the Western Sudan.


C) The Berbers ran productive agricultural plantations; they paid tribute to support the upper echelons of Western Sudanic society.


D) The Berbers dominated the political realms of these states; they were the hereditary kings.A0
B) The Berbers were traders who crossed the desert; they introduced Islam to market towns in the Western Sudan.

The Kingdom of Da'amat was

Question 3 options:

A) a Christian kingdom.


B) built by people from Saba (Yemen) in southern Arabia.


C) linked through trade to southern Arabia and interior regions of Africa.


D) a Muslim empire.
C) linked through trade to southern Arabia and interior regions of Africa.

Christianity was introduced in Aksum

Question 4 options:

A) as a result of war.


B) by Thomas the Apostle.


C) in the 4th century CE.


D) by Coptic traders from Egypt.
C) in the 4th century CE.

How has Ethiopia's environment compared to the environment the rest of the African continent?

Question 5 options:

A) Unlike much of the continent, Ethiopia has remained desert.  Before 600 CE, it was home only to nomadic communities.


B) Like much of the continent, Ethiopia had poor soils and unpredictable rainfall.  Farming was nearly impossible until mechanized farming in the twentieth century CE.


C) Unlike much of the continent, Ethiopia had very fertile, volcanic soils capable of supporting large populations.


D) Similar what was seen across the continent, Ethiopians built their state in the 700s BCE in tropical rainforests.  Ethiopians grew a number of tropical plants, including eggplant and peppers.
C) Unlike much of the continent, Ethiopia had very fertile, volcanic soils capable of supporting large populations.

Great Zimbabwe was not associated with

Question 6 options:

A) the Trans-Saharan trade.


B) growing crops and keeping livestock.


C) monumental architecture with buildings made of stone stacked without mortar.


D) the export of gold and ivory to cities along the East African coast
A) the Trans-Saharan trade.

Timbuktu is associated with

Question 7 options:

A) the Swahili city-states.


B) the Aksum trading empire.


C) Great Zimbabwe.


D) the Western Sudanic empires.
D) the Western Sudanic empires.

All of the following statements were true about Aksum except

Question 8 options:

A) It had its own distinctive Christian Church.


B) It was a great trading empire.


C) It minted its own coinage to facilitate international trade.


D) It was situated on the coast of the Atlantic Ocean.
D) It was situated on the coast of the Atlantic Ocean.

As they described the Bantu migration, 19th century European scholars working in Africa envisioned

Question 9 options:

A) that Bantu speakers would unify to stop the slave trade within the Indian Ocean world.


B) the gradual diffusion of Bantu farming technologies through northern Africa.


C) iron-wielding Bantu speakers rapidly conquering indigenous populations throughout most of eastern and southern Africa.


D) the imminent spread of Islam into the Western Sudanic states.
C) iron-wielding Bantu speakers rapidly conquering indigenous populations throughout most of eastern and southern Africa.

All of the following were true of the Mali Empire except

Question 10 options:

A) Mansa Musa cultivated Mali's relationship with the Islamic world by performing the Hajj, encouraging the use of Arabic, and building new Islamic schools and mosques.


B) Mali built a Trading Post Empire along the East African coast.


C) Mali stretched much farther east, west, and south than had its predecessor kingdom of Ghana.


D) people in Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East recognized Mali as a prosperous trading center.
B) Mali built a Trading Post Empire along the East African coast.

Describe the situation of the Maya in the 9th century CE.

Question 11 options:

A) Many Maya sites collapsed in the 9th century CE. There were famines and many Mayan settlements were largely abandoned.


B) The 9th century CE was the height of Mayan power as the king of Teotihuacán ruled over a unified empire and collected tribute from all neighboring regions.


C) In the 9th century CE, the Maya moved their civilization to what is present-day Colombia.


D) The Mayan culture abruptly ended in the 9th century CE. No Mayan sites remained after the sudden decline of Teotihuacán.
A) Many Maya sites collapsed in the 9th century CE. There were famines and many Mayan settlements were largely abandoned.

Around 10,000 years ago, Mesoamericans began to cultivate squash as both a food source and

Question 12 options:

A) for religious rituals.


B) for storage containers.


C) for constructing their homes.


D) to make fishing threads.
B) for storage containers.

During the Formative Period in Mesoamerica, all of the following occurred except

Question 13 options:

A) the emergence of social differentiation.


B) the creation of large armies.


C) trade linked adjacent regions.


D) the beginnings of urbanization.
B) the creation of large armies.

Archeological findings show that this predated the Olmec production of monumental sculptures.

Question 14 options:

A) The production of stone effigy bowls.


B) The creation of the Olmec Empire.


C) The burial of Olmec elites in large mounds.


D) The construction of sophisticated temples.
A) The production of stone effigy bowls.

Which civilization drew the most attention from the Spanish as they conquered Mexico?

Question 15 options:

A) Olmec


B) Maya


C) Aztec


D) Toltec
C) Aztec

How were kivas used at the Chaco Canyon sites?

Question 16 options:

A) Individual kin groups used them as gathering places.


B) They were a type of pottery used for storage.


C) They were roads that connected neighboring communities.


D) Used to hoe corn, these farming tools increased crop yields.
A) Individual kin groups used them as gathering places.

The first foods domesticated during the Archaic period in Mesoamerica included

Question 17 options:

A) lentils, pineapple, and coffee.


B) beans, squash, and maize.


C) potatoes, sorghum, and okra.


D) wheat, rice, and barley.
B) beans, squash, and maize.

This culture formed the first state in Mesoamerica.

Question 18 options:

A) Maya


B) Olmec


C) Aztec


D) Toltec
B) Olmec

All of the following were civilizations in Mesoamerica except

Question 19 options:

A) Aztec


B) Maya


C) Olmec


D) Moche
D) Moche

Which of the following statements is correct regarding the relationship between the Olmecs, and the Aztecs and the Maya?

Question 20 options:

A) Scholars have not been interested in linking Olmec culture with other cultures found in the region. They believe that the Olmecs were isolated and had no contact with other societies.


B) All scholars agree that the Olmecs served as a "mother culture" for the Aztecs and the Maya.


C) Amongst scholars, there is an ongoing debate about whether the Olmecs were a "mother culture" for the Aztecs and the Maya.


D) The Olmec, Aztec, and Mayan cultures all emerged at the same time.  An overlap in beliefs and practices means that scholars believe that they all came from the same, as yet unidentified, "mother culture."
C) Amongst scholars, there is an ongoing debate about whether the Olmecs were a "mother culture" for the Aztecs and the Maya.

Why did European sailors, particularly Genoese and Iberians, begin exploring the western coast of Africa in the fourteenth century?

Question 1 options:

A) They were looking for a source of coal reserves.


B) They were looking for a city that was said to be the home of a fantastical race of creatures called Chthonians, creatures that, if they could be bribed with gifts of wine, could be convinced to cause earthquakes that could be used against enemies in time of war.


C) They were looking for the legendary Kingdom of Shambala.


D) They were looking for a route to the source of West Africa's gold.
D) They were looking for a route to the source of West Africa's gold.

Which of the following was not of true of daily life in medieval Europe?

Question 2 options:

A) Whereas the noble diet was heavy in meat, peasant farmers never were able to have meat.


B) The most common2 drink in northern Europe was beer.


C) Whereas nobles often resided in large rural houses, peasants usually lived in small houses.


D) Popular noble recreation could result in accidental death.

A) Whereas the noble diet was heavy in meat, peasant farmers never were able to have meat.

This statement is not entirely accurate. While it's true that the noble diet was indeed heavy in meat and peasants had less access to meat compared to nobles, it's not true that peasants never were able to have meat. Peasant diets varied greatly depending on location, time of year, and the resources available to them. In some cases, peasants could have meat, although less frequently and likely in smaller quantities than the nobility. They might have had access to meat through raising their own animals, hunting (where allowed, as some hunting grounds were restricted to nobility), or through communal feasts.

Western Europe's economy began to grow in the late eleventh and twelfth centuries CE because nobles had a demand for luxury goods and

Question 3 options:

A) Italian city-states largely cleared the Mediterranean Sea of Muslim pirates.


B) crops failed in China, and the demand for European grains caused wealth to flow from east to west.


C) when crusaders took the city of Jerusalem, they took control of the treasury of the al-Aqsa Mosque, which had the world's largest known concentration of gold, silver, and precious stones. The gold and silver then entered circulation in European markets.


D) Italian merchants figured out how to make cinnamon, saffron, and cardamom grow in the soil of Western Europe.

A) Italian city-states largely cleared the Mediterranean Sea of Muslim pirates.

The resurgence of trade in luxury goods was facilitated in part by the reduction of piracy and the establishment of safer trade routes, for which Italian city-states like Venice, Genoa, and Pisa played a significant role. This enhanced maritime security in the Mediterranean, allowing for increased trade between Europe and the East, including areas in Asia and the Near East.

After Normans conquered England in 1066, the Kingdom of England

Question 4 options:

A) moved closer to France in language, institutions, and culture, but retained a relatively centralized bureaucracy.


B) became much more politically decentralized as a result of the introduction of feudalism.


C) was annexed to the Kingdom of France.


D) kept nearly all of its indigenous nobility.
A) moved closer to France in language, institutions, and culture, but retained a relatively centralized bureaucracy.

Which of the following was not usually true of a Khagan?

Question 5 options:

A) He maintained group cohesion and warded of challenges through personal charisma and martial skills.


B) He obtained his position by hereditary descent, as eldest son of the prior Khagan.


C) He laid claim to some sort of divine providence.


D) He was primarily the ruler of a steppe confederation of clans and tribes.

B) He obtained his position by hereditary descent, as eldest son of the prior Khagan.

Leadership succession among steppe confederations, such as those led by Khagans, was often not strictly hereditary and could involve a complex mix of election by the nobility, acclamation by the warriors, and consideration of the candidate's prowess, leadership qualities, and sometimes familial ties. The eldest son of the prior Khagan was not always the automatic successor.

In Central Asia, the two ethnic groups most important to the early history of the region were

Question 6 options:

A) Turkic and Mongol.


B) Mongol and Russian.


C) Chinese and Turkic.


D) Persian and Chinese.
A) Turkic and Mongol.

In Western Europe of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, castles

Question 7 options:

A) were almost always made of stone.


B) were initially made of wood, but later came to be constructed out of stone.


C) were initially made of mud brick, but later came to be constructed out of stone.


D) largely served as luxurious houses.

B) were initially made of wood, but later came to be constructed out of stone.

During this period, many castles initially built as wooden structures were rebuilt or replaced with stone to improve their defensive capabilities and durability. The transition from wood to stone made the castles stronger against attacks and fires, reflecting advancements in architecture and the increasing importance of these fortifications in medieval military and social structures.

The name Genghis Khan means

Question 8 options:

A) Fist of Heaven.


B) Flash of the Soaring Dragon of Heaven.


C) universal ruler.


D) sacred and wild.
C) universal ruler.

Which of the following was NOT true of Genghis Khan?

Question 9 options:

A) He believed he was predestined to rule the earth.


B) To become Khagan, he had to overcome many serious obstacles as a youth.


C) He brought a new level of cohesiveness to the diverse tribes of Mongolia.


D) His ascendancy to power culminated with his execution of rivals at a great Mongol assembly held in 1206.

D) His ascendancy to power culminated with his execution of rivals at a great Mongol assembly held in 1206.

While Genghis Khan certainly consolidated his power and united the Mongol tribes, becoming the leader of the Mongols at a great assembly, or kurultai, held in 1206, the statement about executing rivals at this assembly to culminate his ascendancy to power does not accurately describe the historical record of events at that time. The kurultai was more about acknowledging his leadership and establishing his authority rather than a mass execution of rivals. Genghis Khan did systematically eliminate threats to his power, but this was an ongoing process throughout his life, not a single event tied to the kurultai.

When waging war in China, the Mongols faced which challenge?

Question 10 options:

A) The Chinese employed camel-mounted soldiers. These camels could go even further without water than horses, and so could outmaneuver Mongol troops when fighting them in the Gobi Desert.


B) The vast swamps of Northern China meant that the Mongols were unable to use their cavalry for mobility, depriving them of one of their key advantages.


C) Chinese cities were usually walled, and some of these walled cities even connected to smaller fortified towns through a set of underground tunnels.


D) Many Mongol soldiers were Buddhists, and so they were reluctant to engage in violence against the Chinese because many of them were also Buddhists.

C) Chinese cities were usually walled, and some of these walled cities even connected to smaller fortified towns through a set of underground tunnels.

Siege warfare was a significant challenge for the Mongols, who were highly skilled in cavalry and open-field tactics but initially less experienced in taking fortified cities. The walled cities of China required the Mongols to adapt their tactics and to employ engineers and siege technologies, often from conquered peoples, to break through these defenses.

The Islamization of Central Asia

Question 11 options:

A) was prevented by the presence of Chinese garrisons in the region.


B) commenced with the migration of Turkic tribes out of the Orkhon Steppe.


C) began in the twelfth century CE with the rise of the Mongols.


D) began in the eighth century CE and took several centuries.

D) began in the eighth century CE and took several centuries.

The process of Islam's spread into Central Asia started in the eighth century following the Arab conquests that reached the region, but it was a gradual process that unfolded over several centuries, as Islam gradually became the dominant religion through a combination of military conquests, trade, and the work of missionaries.

Although many of the papacy's efforts at Church reform in the eleventh century were successful, one consequence was that

Question 12 options:

A) the Church of England split from the Catholic Church in 1054. It has remained separate from the Catholic Church to the present day.


B) the papacy was forced to relocate to Constantinople for several decades after German troops seized the city of Rome in 1066.


C) the Holy Roman Emperors confiscated all bishops' lands in the Holy Roman Empire. As a result, bishops in the Holy Roman Empire lost a major source of wealth.


D) the Catholic and Orthodox churches were split from one another.

D) the Catholic and Orthodox churches were split from one another.

This describes the Great Schism of 1054, where the Christian church split into what are now known as the Roman Catholic Church in the West and the Orthodox Church in the East. The schism was the culmination of theological and political differences which had developed over the centuries.

The four Crusader States were the County of Edessa, the Principality of Antioch, the Kingdom of Jerusalem, and

Question 13 options:

A) the Duchy of Alexandria.


B) the Commandery of Mt. Athos.


C) the County of Tripoli.


D) the Viceroyalty of New Spain.
C) the County of Tripoli.

As a result of the Third Crusade,

Question 14 options:

A) the crusaders were completely victorious, retaking Jerusalem in 1191 and re-establishing the Kingdom of Jerusalem.


B) the crusaders managed to take northern Egypt, establishing the Duchy of Alexandria.


C) the crusaders were utterly defeated so that all Crusader States came to an end in 1192.


D) the crusaders established control over the coast of the Levant, but were unable to take the city of Jerusalem.

D) the crusaders established control over the coast of the Levant, but were unable to take the city of Jerusalem.

The Third Crusade (1189–1192) led by European monarchs including Richard I of England (Richard the Lionheart), Philip II of France, and Holy Roman Emperor Frederick I, indeed aimed to retake Jerusalem following its capture by Saladin in 1187. However, despite several victories and regaining some territories, the crusaders failed to recapture Jerusalem itself. The crusade concluded with a treaty that allowed Christian pilgrims to visit the city, but it remained under Muslim control.

The result of Manuel Komnenos' efforts to extend the power of the Byzantine Empire was that

Question 15 options:

A) he defeated both the Saljuq Turks and the Fatimid Egyptians, resulting in an Empire that ruled the whole of the Eastern Mediterranean.


B) he was militarily defeated by both the Hungarians and the Saljuq Turks and so it was under his reign that the power of the Byzantine Empire was fully broken.


C) he successfully allied himself with the growing power of the kingdom of Hungary, but was defeated by the Saljuq Turks at the 1176 Battle of Myriokephalon and so was unsuccessful in his efforts to re-assert Byzantine control over central Anatolia.


D) he defeated the Saljuq Turks at the 1176 Battle of Myriokephalon, but was militarily defeated in battle by the Hungarians in the 1184 Battle of Mohi.

C) he successfully allied himself with the growing power of the kingdom of Hungary, but was defeated by the Saljuq Turks at the 1176 Battle of Myriokephalon and so was unsuccessful in his efforts to re-assert Byzantine control over central Anatolia.

Manuel Komnenos (Manuel I Komnenos), an emperor of the Byzantine Empire, sought to restore Byzantine prestige and territory but faced significant challenges. While he did engage in diplomatic and military efforts to extend Byzantine influence, the Battle of Myriokephalon, where his forces were defeated by the Saljuq Turks, marked a significant setback in his campaigns to recover central Anatolia. This battle often marks the end of Byzantine attempts to recover the interior of the peninsula from Turkish control.

Desiderius Erasmus sought to

Question 16 options:

A) use Aristotelian logic to analyze the Greek text of the New Testament.


B) use linguistic analysis on the Sanskrit text of the Ramayana.


C) use Aristotelian logic to analyze the Arabic text of the Quran.


D) use linguistic analysis on the Greek text of the New Testament.

D) use linguistic analysis on the Greek text of the New Testament.

Desiderius Erasmus, a Dutch Renaissance humanist, Catholic priest, scholar, and theologian, was known for his work in classical scholarship and for his efforts to reform the church. One of his significant contributions was the publication of the Greek New Testament in 1516, which he corrected and improved using linguistic and textual analysis. This work was pivotal for the Protestant Reformation, as it provided scholars and theologians direct access to the biblical texts in their original language.

Why was Sufi practice instrumental in helping to convert the peoples of Central Asia to Islam?

Question 17 options:

A) Sufis were doctrinally rigid, and the harsh warriors of the steppe respected their unbending refusal to make concessions in how new converts practiced Islam.


B) Sufis were doctrinally flexible and so made some concessions to pre-Muslim practices among new converts to Islam.


C) Sufis taught that although Muhammad had originally recited the Qur'an in Arabic, the true Qur'an that existed in the Mind of God was in Turkish.


D) Sufis claimed that meditation would give them supernatural powers that would bring victory. The warriors of the steppes thus converted to Islam so that Sufi holy men could perform miracles on the battlefield.

B) Sufis were doctrinally flexible and so made some concessions to pre-Muslim practices among new converts to Islam.

Sufi practices and teachings often emphasized personal, spiritual experiences of the divine, making Sufism appealing and accessible to a wide range of people. Their adaptability and ability to incorporate elements of local traditions into their teachings helped facilitate the spread of Islam in culturally diverse areas, including Central Asia. This approach allowed Sufism to serve as a bridge, integrating Islamic spiritual practices with the existing cultural and religious traditions of newly converted peoples.

This ruler sought to impede the decentralization of power in the Holy Roman Empire.

Question 18 options:

A) Frederick Barbarossa


B) Manuel Komnenos


C) Timur


D) King Edward I

A) Frederick Barbarossa

Frederick Barbarossa, who was Holy Roman Emperor from 1155 until his death in 1190, actively worked to strengthen imperial authority and impede the decentralization of power within the Holy Roman Empire. He sought to consolidate the empire's territories and reduce the power of autonomous dukes and princes, aiming for a more centralized imperial rule.

The term Reconquista refers to

Question 19 options:

A) efforts by Spanish Christians to conquer the Muslim-ruled Middle East and subject it to Spanish rule.


B) efforts by Spanish Christians to reconquer Muslim-ruled Spain and subject it to Christian rule.


C) efforts by Spanish Muslims to reconquer Christian-ruled Spain and subject it to Muslim rule.


D) efforts by the Spanish to reconquer Spain from its French rulers.

B) efforts by Spanish Christians to reconquer Muslim-ruled Spain and subject it to Christian rule.

The Reconquista is the period in the history of the Iberian Peninsula, spanning approximately 780 years between the initial Islamic invasion in 711 and the fall of Granada, the last Muslim state on the peninsula, to the Christian forces of King Ferdinand II of Aragon and Queen Isabella I of Castile in 1492. This era is characterized by numerous wars and battles between Christian states in the north of the peninsula and the Muslim states in the south, which were collectively known as Al-Andalus. The ultimate goal of the Christian kingdoms was the recovery of territories held by Muslims, a process completed with the conquest of Granada.

The most significant geographical feature of Central Asia is

Question 20 options:

A) the Great Sahara Desert, a massive set of deserts that have long prevented the people of Central Asia from having much contact with Europe.


B) the Great Vilayet Sea, an inland sea that stretches for over a thousand miles from what is now Russia into Northern Iran.


C) the Dead Marshes, a set of swamps stretching for hundreds of miles in every direction. They are known as the Dead Marshes because the bodies of those slain by Genghis Khan in 1206 and ordered thrown into the swamp can still be seen today.


D) the Great Eurasian Steppe, a set of broad, flat grasslands that reach from Eastern Europe to the region of Mongolia to the north of China.

D) the Great Eurasian Steppe, a set of broad, flat grasslands that reach from Eastern Europe to the region of Mongolia to the north of China.

The Great Eurasian Steppe is indeed the most significant geographical feature of Central Asia. This vast expanse of grasslands has been crucial historically for the movement of peoples, goods, and ideas across the continent. It facilitated the spread of nomadic cultures and was the route for significant historical events such as the migrations and invasions of various nomadic groups, including the Huns, the Mongols, and the Turks.
